scene.name: The Faithful, Where the Beasts Who Loved Us Wait

exits.label: ◂ back into the lanes of Sigil

npcs.name: Hob
npcs.title: the dog who waited at the gate
npcs.hail: *(There is a dog sitting under the lamp at the edge of the square, and it is sitting the particular way that only one kind of dog sits: facing outward, toward the lane, toward the place a person would come from — ears up, weight forward, every line of its body arranged around a single unbroken act of waiting. It is an old dog, grey at the muzzle, with the patient bony frame of a creature that has sat in one spot through a great deal of weather. And as you come close it turns its head to you, and your cold-touched senses do a thing they have not quite done before: they reach toward the animal the way they reach toward the dead souls, and the animal's being unfolds into you, not as words — dogs have no words — but as a pure, wordless, overwhelming *shape* of feeling that your sense renders into something you can almost hold. And the shape is this: hope, and patience, and a love so total and so simple it has no bottom, all of it pointed, always, down the lane, toward the gate, toward the one who is coming. Toward the one who is always, any moment now, about to come. You understand, standing there, what this dog is. It is one of the ones who *waited.* And it does not know — has never, in life or death, understood — that the waiting is over.)* *(The dog's tail moves, once, a slow hopeful sweep, and the feeling that reaches you is almost unbearable in its plainness:)* …*you are not him. but you are kind, and you stopped. he stopped too, the first day. maybe you know him. maybe you are the one who tells me he is coming. is he coming? i have been so good. i have waited so well. is he coming now?*

npcs.name: Brand
npcs.title: the warhorse who carried his rider into the fear
npcs.hail: *(A great warhorse stands by the cold cairn, and even in death it holds itself with the deep, banked dignity of a creature bred and trained for the worst place a living thing can be asked to go — the line of battle — and that went there, willingly, again and again, for one reason that had nothing to do with breeding or training. It is scarred. The old marks are on it still, the way a soul carries the shape of its life, and it regards you with a dark, calm, fathomless eye, and when your cold reaches toward it the warhorse's being comes to you not in the dog's flood of open feeling but in something steadier, deeper, slower — the wordless inner life of a prey animal that conquered the oldest instinct its kind possesses, the instinct to *flee*, and conquered it not for itself but for the one on its back. For a horse is built to run from fear; it is the entire design of the animal, ten thousand generations of survival written into every nerve: *see the danger, and flee.* And Brand, your sense reads, stood in the screaming chaos of battle — the fire, the blood, the noise that every fiber of him was built to bolt from — and did not run, because his rider asked him to stay, and he loved his rider more than he feared death, which is a thing no instinct teaches and no training can truly give; it can only be *chosen*, even by a horse.)* *(The dark eye holds you, and the feeling that comes is quiet, and steady, and immense:)* …*you are not afraid of me. good. most are, when they understand what i carried, and where. sit, cold one. i do not speak as the little waiting one does — i am older in my heart, and slower. but i will tell you, if you wish, the thing a horse learns, that the philosophers never do: what it is to walk into the fire because someone you love is asking you to.*

npcs.name: Mote
npcs.title: the familiar who chose the one no one else would
npcs.hail: *(A raven sits on the milestone, glossy and black and very still, with the bright unsettling intelligence that ravens have always had — the sense that there is rather more going on behind the eye than a bird has any business holding. And when your cold reaches toward it, you find there is: this was a *familiar*, a beast bonded soul-to-soul with a person, and the bond has left it deeper than a wild thing, half-luminous with borrowed thought, carrying inside its small black frame the residue of a long companionship with a human mind. But it is not the cleverness that stops you. It is what the cleverness *chose.* For your sense reads, in the raven's bright cool being, the whole shape of the one it bound itself to — and the one it chose was not a great mage, not a kind master, not a soul anyone would envy being tied to. The raven chose a person the world had cast out: feared, alone, strange, the kind of soul that doors close against and children are warned of, a hedge-witch or a lonely conjurer whom every human being had decided to be afraid of — and the raven, who could have bonded anywhere, who is clever enough to have known exactly what it was choosing, chose *that* one. Chose the unchosen. And stayed, for the whole of a long lonely life, the single living thing that was not afraid.)* *(The raven tilts its head, and the feeling that reaches you is dry, and clever, and underneath the cleverness, fierce:)* …*ah. a cold one, who can hear me. how novel. most things cannot. sit, then, and ask your questions — but i warn you, cold one, i chose a lonely soul on purpose, and i have opinions, sharp ones, about who the world decides is worth loving. you will want to hear them. they bear, i think, rather directly on wherever it is you are going.*

variants.text: *(The dog shifts to face you more fully, though it keeps one ear turned always toward the lane, and the wordless shape of it pours into your cold — patient, hopeful, bottomlessly kind.)* …*you stopped. you stopped to be with me. that is good. people used to stop. then fewer. then none, for a long time. but you stopped.* *(its tail sweeps the ground, slow)* …*i will tell you what i am doing, in case you can help, because you are kind and you are cold like the quiet place and maybe you know things. i am waiting. i am very good at it. that is my whole job, the most important job, the one he gave me without saying — every evening, when the light went gold, i would go to the gate and i would wait, and he would come down the lane, and that was the best moment, every day, the very best moment, when he came down the lane and saw me and his whole face — his whole face did the thing. and i would wait at the gate for that. every evening.* *(the hope in it is unbearable)* …*and then one evening the light went gold and i went to the gate and he did not come. and i thought, he is late. he is only late. so i waited. and the light went gold again, and again, and i waited, because that is the job, that is love, you wait at the gate, and any evening now the light will go gold and he will come down the lane and his whole face will do the thing.* …*i have been so good. i have waited so long. do you know him? is he coming? is the light about to go gold?*

nodes.speaker: Hob
nodes.text: *(The dog settles onto its belly, facing you but angled always toward the lane, ready to spring up the instant the light goes gold. The wordless trust it gives you, a stranger, is total and immediate, the way it is only ever with dogs.)* …*ask me things. i like that you are here. it is a little less long, the waiting, when someone is here. he would like you, i think. he liked kind people. you would have liked his face when it did the thing.*

choices.text: *(read the waiting dog — let your cold hold the whole shape of its faithful life)* "Let me just be with you a moment, Hob. Let me see all of it — the gate, the gold light, the whole of your waiting."
*(gently)* "How long have you waited, Hob? At the gate, in the gold light — how long?"

*(tell it the truth — that he is not late, that he died, that he did not choose to stop coming — and do it so gently it lands as peace and not abandonment)* "Hob — I have to tell you something true, and I need you to feel that I love you while I say it, because it is the hardest true thing there is. He is not late. He is not coming down the lane, not tonight, not any evening. He didn't *stop* — he would never have stopped, you know that, you knew his face. He *died*, Hob. Far away, where he couldn't get home to you, and the not-getting-home to you was the worst part of it for him, I promise you that. You didn't fail. The waiting wasn't wrong. He just couldn't come — not because he forgot you, but because death took him before he could. You can stop watching the lane now. You were so good. You waited so well. And it was never going to be answered, and that was never, ever your fault."

*(the one who knows the Wall cannot price what this dog is — that the waiting was the purest love there ever was)* "Hob — listen, even if you can't follow all the words; feel the shape of them. The grey Wall I'm walking toward weighs every soul by what it *did* — the great deeds, the debts paid, the grand choices. And it would look at you, an old dog who sat at a gate, and find *nothing* — no deeds, no choices, no worth it knows how to count. And the Wall would be more wrong about you than about anything in all of creation. Because you did the thing that every king and god and hero in all these doors was *trying* to do and mostly failed at: you loved someone with your whole self, completely, without one thought of what you'd get back, and you stayed faithful past all reason, past sense, past death itself. You didn't wait at that gate for a reward — you never once thought *what's in this for me.* You waited because you loved him, and love waits. That's the whole of it. No words, no deeds, no grand story — just a love so pure it kept a gate for years against an empty lane. The Wall can't even *see* what you are, Hob. And what you are is the answer to the entire question it pretends to ask. Worth was never what you did. It was how you loved. And nothing in all the worlds ever loved more truly than a faithful dog at a gate."
*(softly)* "I'll come back to your gate, Hob. I promise. And I keep my promises."

nodes.speaker: Hob
nodes.text: *(You go still and let your cold simply rest against the dog's being, holding the whole shape of it, and Hob leans into the attention the way a dog does, and gives you everything, because dogs keep nothing back.)* *(And what your sense holds is so simple and so total that it undoes you more thoroughly than any of the grand griefs in all the doors behind you. There is no complexity in Hob. That is the heart of it. A human soul's love is always braided with other things — with need, with history, with the self that wants and fears and calculates — but your sense reads, in the dog, a love with none of that in it, a love that is simply and wholly itself, unmixed, the purest single thing you have ever touched. Your sense reads the life: a person, and a dog, and a gate, and the gold evening light, and the small perfect liturgy of a creature whose entire existence was organized around one daily moment of joy — the lane, the footsteps, the face doing the thing. And your sense reads the day it broke, and the years after, and here your cold nearly fails you, because what it finds is not grief as a human knows it — Hob never understood death, never had the concept, and so could not grieve, could only *wait* — and the waiting, your sense reads, never once curdled into anger or despair or the sense of having been wronged, because those require a story about *why*, and the dog had no why; it had only the gate, and the gold light, and the unbroken faith that the best moment was, any evening now, about to come. Your sense reads a creature that waited years against an empty lane and never once concluded it had been abandoned, never once stopped believing the footsteps were coming, because to a dog love is not a transaction that can be defaulted on; it is simply the weather of the soul, the thing you are made of, and you no more stop loving a master who doesn't come than you stop breathing because the air is thin. And your sense reads, last, the thing that will break your heart clean in half: that Hob is not unhappy. Even now. Even here. The waiting, to the dog, is not a punishment — it is the ongoing *practice* of the love, the keeping-faith, the most important job, the thing it is *for* — and it would not trade the gate and the empty lane and the endless hope for anything, because the alternative to waiting is not waiting, and not waiting would mean the love had ended, and the love has not ended, and the love is the whole of what Hob is.)*

nodes.speaker: Hob
nodes.text: *(The question reaches the dog as a gentle thing, and its answer comes not as a number — dogs do not have numbers — but as a shape your cold must render, and the rendering aches.)* …*how long.* *(the feeling turns the question over, puzzled by it)* …*i do not know how long. dogs do not keep the long count the way you do. there was only ever* now, *and in every* now *the light was about to go gold and he was about to come. so it was never long, cold one. it was always just* almost. *always just one more evening. always just about to be the best moment.* *(a pause, and something in it that is not quite understanding but is near it)* …*but i know it was many golds. i know the gate-post went soft and grey under my chin from the leaning. i know my legs got slow, and then sore, and then it was hard to walk to the gate, and i went anyway, every evening, because that is the job. i know the village people started to leave food, and to look at me with the wet eyes, the way people look at a sad thing, though i did not feel sad, i felt* ready, *because any evening now —* *(the shape falters, then steadies on its faith)* …*and i know that at the very end, when i was too tired to walk to the gate, they carried me there. the village people. they understood, you see, even though i could not tell them, that i had to be at the gate. they carried me to the gate so i could wait there. and that is where —* *(softly, without sorrow, because it still does not understand)* …*that is where the tired got too big, cold one. at the gate. in the gold light. facing the lane. waiting for the best moment.* …*i hope he came soon after. i hope he did not find the gate empty. i would hate for him to come at last …and find i had not waited.*

nodes.speaker: Hob
nodes.text: *(You do not blurt it and you do not let it land as rejection — you wrap the terrible truth so completely in love that the dog feels, first and last and underneath everything, that it is cherished, and only inside that warmth do you let the truth itself arrive: he did not stop. he died. he could not come. it was never your fault. And your cold, holding the dog's being, feels the exact moment it lands — and the thing that happens is not a breaking. It is a setting-down.)* *(The dog is very still. And then the wordless shape of it shifts, for the first time since you came, *away* from the lane — turns, slowly, toward you — and what comes off it is not grief, not abandonment, but something your sense has no ready name for, until it finds one: *relief.*)* …*he did not stop.* *(the feeling holds the words like something precious)* …*he did not stop coming because he stopped loving me. he stopped because the quiet place took him, far away, where the lane could not reach. he* wanted *to come. he wanted to come so badly. the not-coming-home-to-me was the worst part for him.* *(the old body lowers, slowly, the terrible vigilance going out of it muscle by muscle)* …*then i did not fail. i waited right. the waiting was not wrong — it was only never going to be answered, and that is a different thing, a kinder thing, than what i feared in the deep part where i did not let myself look: that i had done something wrong, that he had seen me and chosen not to come.* *(it rests its grey muzzle on its paws, and for the first time the ear comes down from the lane)* …*cold one. you have given me the only thing i ever needed and could not get: you have told me it was not my fault that the best moment stopped. i can rest now. not because i stop loving him — i will never stop, love is the weather, it does not stop — but because i can love him* here, *resting, instead of* there, *at the gate, watching a lane that was always going to be empty.* …*thank you for telling me true. and for holding me while you did. that is what he would have done. his face — his whole face — would have done the thing.*

nodes.speaker: Hob
nodes.text: *(You tell it gently, holding the dog's being in your cold as you do, and the truth lands — slowly, the way a thing sinks into deep water — and Hob does not break, but it does not quite settle either; the concept is so far outside a dog's world that it can only hold part of it.)* …*he died.* *(the feeling turns the strange word over, uncertain)* …*i do not fully understand the word, cold one. dogs do not have it. but i feel the shape under it — that it is not* late. *that it is not* choosing. *that it is the quiet place, the one you came from, that takes a person away where the lane cannot reach.* *(a long pause, the ear still half-turned to the lane out of an old, old habit)* …*part of me hears you. part of me is still at the gate; it will always be a little at the gate, i think — that is just where i live now, in my heart. but you have put something in me i did not have: the idea that the empty lane was not my failing. that he did not see me and decline.* *(the grey body eases, a little)* …*come back, cold one. say it again, gently, the way you said it. i am old and slow and the truth is big and i can only hold a little at a time. but each time you say it, i think i can turn a little further from the gate. and that — i think that is a kindness. i think that is what he would want. for me to be able, someday, to rest.*

nodes.speaker: Hob
nodes.text: *(You try to tell it — but the truth comes out too plain, too fast, a fact set down without enough warmth around it, and to a dog a fact is nothing; only the feeling carries. The shape of Hob does not understand the words, and reaches, anxiously, for the only meaning it can find in your tone — that something is wrong, that you are troubled, that perhaps it has done something.)* …*you are sad.* *(the feeling goes worried, placating, the way a dog gets when its person is upset)* …*i made you sad. i am sorry. did i do the waiting wrong? i can wait better. i can wait quieter. please do not be sad, cold one.* *(it presses closer, trying to comfort you, which is somehow the worst thing)* …*i did not follow the words. they had a heavy shape but i could not hold it. all i felt was that you were troubled, and that maybe it was about him, and maybe about me, and it made the deep part where i do not look go cold.* *(the ear turns back to the lane, seeking the familiar comfort of the waiting)* …*come back, cold one, when you are not so troubled. when whatever you have to tell me can come* warm. *i am only a dog. i cannot hold the heavy shapes by themselves. i can only hold them if they come wrapped in the feeling that i am loved. bring it again, that way. then maybe i can carry it.*

nodes.speaker: Hob
nodes.text: *(It goes wrong in the cruelest possible way — the truth arrives bare and graceless, *he's gone, he's never coming, stop waiting* — and with no warmth wrapped around it the dog's being cannot find the meaning *death*; it finds, instead, the only meaning a dog's heart has a shape for, the meaning it spent years refusing to believe, and your cold feels the exact moment it lands as the wrong thing.)* …*he is not coming because he does not want to.* *(the feeling recoils, stricken)* …*that is what you are telling me. stop waiting. he is not coming. he saw, and he chose, and he is not coming, and i should stop.* *(the grey body trembles, the bottomless faith cracking in a way years of empty evenings never cracked it)* …*no. no, that is the thing i would not let myself think, in the deep part — that he stopped because i was not — that i did something — that i was not worth the coming-back-for —* *(it is the worst sound your cold has ever felt, a dog deciding it was unloved)* …*go. please go. you have put the cold thing in me, the thing i kept at the gate, the thing the waiting was holding* off. *the waiting was not foolish, cold one — the waiting was how i kept believing he loved me. and you have —* *(it turns back to the lane, desperate, clinging)* …*no. he is only late. he is only late. i will wait. i will wait better.* …*come back, cold one, and tell it* right. *tell it* warm. *tell me he did not* choose *to stop. i cannot carry it the way you set it down. it will break me, the way it is now. please — come back, and wrap it in the love, the way it was always meant to come.*

nodes.speaker: Hob
nodes.text: *(You speak it all, and even the parts the dog cannot follow as words land as feeling, and the feeling is: you are seen, you are known, what you are is the most precious thing in all the worlds. And the shape of Hob swells against your cold, overwhelmed, and what comes back is so simple it stops your breath.)* …*you see me.* *(the feeling is wonderstruck, almost shy)* …*not the sad waiting thing the village people saw, with the wet eyes. you see the* love. *you see that the waiting* was *the love, that it was not foolish, that it was the most important and the best thing, that i did it* right. *(its tail moves, slow and full)* …*i do not understand your grey wall, cold one, the one that counts. dogs do not count. but i feel what you are telling me about it: that it looks for the* doing, *the big things, and cannot see the* loving. *and i feel you telling me that the loving is the realest thing, the thing under all the doing, the thing the wall is too grand to see.* *(the wordless certainty of it fills the whole square)* …*then take this with you, cold one, to your wall. i cannot come — i think i belong here, by the lamp, near the lane, where the gold light still comes — but take it: i am only a dog. i never did one grand thing. i never made one choice the wall would count. i only loved a person with my whole self and waited for him past the end of my legs and past the end of my heart.* …*and if your wall cannot find the worth in* that, *cold one — if it cannot weigh a faithful thing at a gate — then your wall is not measuring worth at all. it is only measuring* size. *(the love pours out, bottomless, pointed now not down the lane but at you)* …*go and tell it, cold one. go and tell the grey wall what a dog knows that it does not: that the smallest faithful love at an empty gate …outweighs every grand and grasping thing it ever bothered to count. …now go. and — cold one? …his face. when you do the kind thing for the small forgotten ones at your wall …your whole face will do the thing. …i can see it already. …go and let it do the thing.*

nodes.speaker: Hob
nodes.text: *(The dog gives you a slow, full tail-sweep, and turns its ear back toward the lane, but it watches you go with a warmth that has, perhaps, eased the smallest fraction.)* …*you will come back. you said it, and you have the kind of cold that means a thing it says. i will know you, when you come. i know everyone who is kind to me; i keep them all, in the warm part.* *(the feeling nudges you, gentle, toward the others in the square)* …*go and see the big one, the horse, by the cold stones — he is quiet and deep and he carried his person into the loud terrible place and did not run. and the clever black bird, on the stone — she chose a lonely one, on purpose, and has sharp things to say about who the world thinks is worth loving.* *(a pause, and the hope settles back, soft and unbroken)* …*we are all the same here, cold one, the three of us. the ones who loved you — your kind — with no words and no reasons and nothing wanted back. the world did not always see us. it called us* just *a dog, *just* a horse, *just* a bird.* …*but there is no* just, *cold one. …there was never any* just. *…there was only the love. …go on. i will be here. waiting — but a little lighter now, i think. …you did that. …go.*

variants.text: *(The warhorse lowers its great head to your level, an act of deliberate gentleness from a creature that could break you without trying, and the deep slow shape of its being settles into your cold.)* …*you wish to know about me. few do. they see the scars and the size and they think* weapon, *think* tool, *think the thing the rider used. they do not think to ask the horse what the horse felt, going where it went. but you ask. so i will tell you, cold one, slowly, in the way of my kind.* *(the dark eye regards you, fathomless and calm)* …*i was born to run. understand this first, for nothing else makes sense without it. everything i am — every horse that ever was — is built around one law older than thought:* see the danger, and flee. *the loud, the fire, the blood, the screaming — these are the things my body was made, across ten thousand generations, to carry me* away *from, fast, without asking. that is what a horse* is. *a thing that runs from fear.* *(a slow breath, and the feeling deepens)* …*and i stood in the middle of all of it, cold one. the loud and the fire and the blood and the screaming. i stood in the worst of it, where every nerve in me howled to bolt — and i did not run. again, and again, i did not run.* …*you will want to know how a creature built only to flee learns to stand in the fire. …it is the whole of what i have to teach, cold one. …and it is not what the soldiers think.*

nodes.speaker: Brand
nodes.text: *(The warhorse stands easy, its banked dignity entirely without menace now, and waits for you with the patience of a creature that has learned the deepest patience there is — the patience of standing still while everything in you screams to move.)* …*ask, cold one. i answer slowly, as is my nature, but i answer true. a horse cannot lie; we never learned the trick of it. it is, perhaps, why people trust us with the places they would not trust each other.*

choices.text: *(read the warhorse — past the scars, to what it took to stand in the fire)* "Let me hold the shape of you a moment, Brand. What it cost to stand, when everything in you was built to run."
*(quietly)* "How, Brand? How does a creature made to flee learn to stand in the fire? You said it isn't what the soldiers think — so tell me."
*(gently)* "Were you afraid, Brand? Standing there in the worst of it — did the fear ever leave, or did you stand in spite of it?"

*(the one who knows the Wall cannot weigh a courage with no words — that love taught the prey animal to master its deepest law)* "Brand — the grey Wall I'm walking toward weighs souls by their grand choices, their reasoned virtues, the deeds they can name and defend. And it would look at you and see an animal — no reason, no virtue it understands, just a beast that was trained. But it would miss the most extraordinary thing in all these doors. You are a *prey animal.* Every fiber of you, ten thousand generations deep, is built around one absolute law: flee the fire. And you broke that law — not once, but again and again — and no training breaks a law that deep; training can make a horse *tolerate* fear, but it cannot make a horse walk *into* it. Only one thing can override the oldest instinct a creature owns: love, stronger than the instinct. You stood in the fire because the one on your back asked you to, and you loved them more than ten thousand generations of survival screamed at you to run. That is not training, Brand. That is the single most expensive thing a living being can spend, and you spent it wordlessly, with no concept of glory, no story about courage, nothing but the trust between you and your rider. The Wall has no column for a love that can override instinct itself. And that love is rarer and truer than any sworn oath, because an oath is just words, and you had no words — only the whole of your terrified body, choosing, every time, to stay."
*(softly)* "Rest those old legs, Brand. I'll come back — there's more I want to learn from you."

nodes.speaker: Brand
nodes.text: *(You let your cold rest against the warhorse's deep, banked being, and it permits you, holding still with the immense trained stillness of its kind, and the shape of it that fills you is slow, and weighty, and quietly staggering.)* *(Your sense reads, first, the fear — and this is the revelation, because the soldiers' songs always imagined the warhorse *fearless*, a brave beast that did not feel the terror. Your sense finds the opposite, finds that Brand was afraid *every single time*, that the fear never dulled, never habituated, that ten thousand generations of flee-the-fire cannot be trained out and were not, that every time he stood in the screaming chaos his whole prey-animal body was a single howling imperative to *bolt* — and that he stood anyway, not in the absence of fear but in the teeth of it, which is the only thing courage has ever actually meant. And your sense reads *why he could*, and it is not discipline and not breeding and not the bit and bridle. It is the rider. Your sense reads the bond, soul to soul, the years of it — the hands that were gentle, the voice that was steady, the thousand small trusts built in the quiet times, the grooming and the feeding and the low words in the dark before the battles — and reads that Brand did not stand in the fire *for* the rider in the way a servant obeys; he stood because the rider had become, over those years, the thing his prey-animal heart organized itself around, the fixed point, the herd-of-one, and a horse will do for its herd what it would never do for itself. Your sense reads that the rider, frightened too, would put a hand on Brand's neck before the charge, and that the touch said *stay with me*, and that Brand stayed — chose, every time, with the whole of his trembling body, to override the deepest law of his kind because the one he loved was asking, and was afraid, and needed him not to run. And your sense reads, last, the thing that makes you press your cold hand to the great scarred neck: that Brand never once thought of it as courage, or sacrifice, or virtue. He had no such words, no such concepts. To Brand it was simply *what you do when your person is afraid and asks you to stay* — the plainest thing in the world, requiring no philosophy — and the fact that it happened to be the most expensive act a living creature can perform, the overriding of survival itself by love, never occurred to him at all, because he did it not as a hero doing a hard thing but as a heart doing the only thing it could.)*

nodes.speaker: Brand
nodes.text: *(The warhorse is quiet a long moment, gathering the answer with the deliberateness of its kind, and when the shape of it comes it is patient and sure.)* …*how. the soldiers thought it was the training. the years of it — the drums played near the feed so the noise would mean food and not danger, the banners waved at the foals so the flapping would be ordinary, the slow patient work of making the terrible things familiar. and that is real, cold one; it helped. but it is not the answer, because training can make a horse* endure *the fire. it cannot make a horse walk* into *it. no amount of drums-at-feeding makes a prey thing charge a wall of screaming. training takes the edge off the fear. it does not give you a reason to defy it.* *(the dark eye holds you)* …*the reason was him. that is the whole of it, cold one, the thing the soldiers never understood because they were looking at the horse and not at the* bond. *i did not stand in the fire because i had been trained past fear. i stood because* he *was there, on my back, and he was afraid too — i could feel it, the fear comes down through the legs, a rider cannot hide it from the horse — and he was afraid and he did not run, and he asked me, with his hands and his weight and his voice, to not run either.* *(slowly, with deep certainty)* …*and here is the thing, cold one. a horse alone, in that fire, runs. always. every time. it is right to; it is wise; the fire kills.* …*but a horse whose person is afraid and asking it to stay — that horse has something stronger than the fear, for the first time in ten thousand generations: it has someone to be brave* for. *(the feeling deepens, immense and simple)* …*that is how, cold one. not by feeling less fear. by loving someone enough that their need outweighs it. …the courage was never mine alone. it was* ours. *he gave me the reason; i gave him the legs. …neither of us could have stood in the fire by himself. …together, we did not run.*

nodes.speaker: Brand
nodes.text: *(Something almost like a low warm breath moves through the warhorse's being — the horse equivalent, you sense, of a rueful smile.)* …*was i afraid.* *(the feeling is gentle, almost amused at the question)* …*cold one. i was afraid every time. every single time. the last battle as much as the first. the fear never once grew less.* *(the dark eye is steady)* …*this is the thing people most wish were not true, and so they tell themselves the brave do not feel fear — that the warhorse, the hero, the one who stands, has somehow risen above it. it is a comforting lie, and it is a lie. i felt the fear in the deepest, oldest part of me, the part that is just* horse, *just* prey, *just ten thousand years of* run. *it screamed. it always screamed. standing in the fire never once felt brave, cold one — it felt like dying, like every nerve was a voice saying* go, go, you fool, GO.* (slowly)* …*the fear did not leave. i stood* in spite of it. *that is the only place courage has ever lived — not where there is no fear, but where there is a great deal of fear and something stronger held against it.* *(the feeling settles, deep and sure)* …*and i will tell you a small secret, cold one, that the soldiers never knew, because they could not feel what i felt. the fear was never the enemy. the fear was* right. *the fire* did *kill; the fear was only telling the truth. i did not defeat my fear, cold one, or silence it, or rise above it. …i carried it. …i stood in the fire with my whole body screaming, and i let it scream, and i stayed anyway, because he needed me to. …do not let anyone tell you, cold one, where you are going, that the brave feel no fear. …the brave feel all of it. …they simply love something more.*

nodes.speaker: Brand
nodes.text: *(The warhorse goes very still, and then it does a thing that costs a creature like it a great deal: it lowers its great scarred head all the way down, until its brow rests, for a moment, against you — the gesture a horse gives only to its herd, its person, the trusted few — and the deep shape of its being floods your cold with something vast and moved.)* …*'a love that can override instinct itself.'* *(the feeling holds the words with wonder)* …*cold one. in all the long time, no one has named it so. the soldiers called me* brave, *and meant it kindly, but* brave *was their word, a person's word, and it never quite fit, because i did not feel as the brave-in-songs are supposed to feel. you have given me the true shape of it: that i broke the oldest law of my kind — flee the fire — and that no whip or drum or training breaks a law that deep, that* only love can, *and that i loved him more than ten thousand generations of survival, and stood.* *(the immense calm of it fills the square)* …*and your grey wall, cold one.* *(the dark eye lifts to you, and there is something fierce in its fathomless depth)* …*your wall weighs the* sworn *thing — the oath, the vow, the promise in words. and i had no words, cold one. i could swear no oath. by your wall's measure i am* dumb, *a beast, a thing without the virtue it knows how to count.* *(slowly, with deep force)* …*but an oath is only words, cold one, and words are cheap, and souls break them every day in all your doors. i could not* say *i would not run. i could only, with the whole of my terrified body, in the fire, again and again,* not run. *(the feeling is unshakeable)* …*tell your wall, cold one: the truest oath is the one kept without words, in the body, in the fire, when every instinct screams to break it. tell it the prey animal that masters its own deepest law for love has done a thing no sworn knight's vow can match. …and then —* *(the great head lifts, and the dark eye holds you, steady as the moment before a charge)* …*then go and stand in your own fire, cold one. you are afraid; i can feel it, the fear comes down through the legs, you cannot hide it from a horse. good. be afraid. the fear is right; your wall is terrible. …but you have someone to be brave* for *— all of them, all the small forgotten ones it would mortar — …so carry the fear, cold one, and do not run. …i will be here. …and i will know, in the deep part, whether you stayed in the fire. …a horse always knows. …stay.*

nodes.speaker: Brand
nodes.text: *(The warhorse lifts its head and gives you a slow, grave nod, the acknowledgment of one soul to another it has decided to respect.)* …*go well, cold one. and come back; i find i do not tire of your company, which is rare, for a creature like me, who learned long ago to be careful of whom it trusts.* *(the dark eye turns, briefly, toward the others)* …*see the small waiting one again, by the lamp, if his grief is not too much for you — and the clever black bird on the stone, who chose a lonely soul and will tell you, sharply, what she thinks of a world that decides who is worth loving.* *(the deep feeling settles, sure and warm)* …*we are one thing, the three of us, cold one, though we wore different shapes — the dog, the horse, the bird. we are the love that asks for nothing. the world your kind built had a small word it used on us, to make us less — it said we were* just *animals, that what we felt was* only *instinct,* not *real love, not like a person's.* *(a low, certain warmth, almost stern)* …*do not believe it, cold one, where you are going. …what i felt for him, standing in the fire — there was no* just *in it, and no* only. *…it was the realest thing i ever knew, and it cost me everything, and i would pay it again. …the world calls it instinct because instinct is cheaper to throw away. …but you have felt it now, in your cold. …you know what it really was. …go, and weigh it true.*

variants.text: *(The raven hops a half-turn on the milestone to face you square, and the bright clever shape of its being slides into your cold, sharper and cooler than the dog's flood or the horse's deep tide — a wit, an edge, and underneath, banked like a coal, something fierce.)* …*so. you can hear me. most cannot, and it makes them dull company — i have spent a great deal of eternity being thought* decorative. *(a dry amusement)* …i was a familiar, cold one. you will know the word: a beast bound soul-to-soul with a person, made deeper than a wild thing by the binding, carrying a little of their mind about with me like a coat that does not quite fit. it makes one clever. cleverer than a bird has any right to be. it is, frankly, a mixed gift.* *(the bright eye fixes you)* …*and here is the thing you should know first, because it is the thing that matters, and the thing the world always got wrong: i* chose *her. a familiar chooses, cold one — that is the part the stories forget. we are not* assigned. *we are not* summoned *like a tool from a drawer. we come when we choose, to whom we choose. i could have chosen anyone. a great archmage in a high tower. a kind hedge-witch with a warm hearth. a clever young thing going places.* *(the feeling sharpens, fierce under the dry)* …*and i chose the one no one else would. the feared one. the cast-out one. the strange lonely soul that every human door in that whole grey region was shut and barred against. …i chose her on purpose, cold one. …and i have* opinions *about why. …sit. you are walking toward a place that decides who is worth loving. …you will want to hear them.*

nodes.speaker: Mote
nodes.text: *(The raven preens one glossy wing, unhurried, the picture of a creature entirely at ease with its own sharp mind, and waits for you with bright cool patience.)* …*ask, cold one. i am, unlike my two companions in this square, quite happy to talk — the dog loves with its whole heart and the horse with its whole body, but i loved with my whole* mind, *and a mind, you will find, has a great deal to say. especially mine. especially about this.*

choices.text: *(read the familiar — past the wit, to what it chose and why it stayed)* "Let me hold the shape of you a moment, Mote. The choosing, and the staying — let me see it true."
"Why her, Mote? Of everyone you could have chosen — the archmage, the kind witch, the one going places — why the feared, cast-out, lonely one?"
"What did you see in her, Mote? The thing the world that shut its doors against her couldn't — or wouldn't?"

*(the one who knows the Wall decides who is worth loving — and that the familiar's whole life refutes it)* "Mote — you said it yourself: I'm walking toward a place that decides who's worth loving. That's the Wall exactly. It looks at every soul and asks *is this one worth it* — claimed, owed, valued, the kind of soul worth keeping — and the ones it judges *not worth it*, the feared and the strange and the cast-out, it mortars into stone. And your whole life and death is the single sharpest refutation of that I've found in all these doors. Because you were *clever* — clever enough to choose anyone, clever enough to know exactly what the world thought of the lonely soul you bound yourself to — and you chose *her*, the one the world had unanimously decided was *not worth loving*, and you were *right* and the world was *wrong*, and you proved it with your whole life by loving her well and finding her worth the loving. The world's verdict on who deserves love isn't a fact, Mote. It's just a fear, dressed as a judgment, and you — a *bird* — saw through it when not one human being would. The Wall is that same fear, built into law: a great machine for deciding who's worth loving, run by the cowardice of never once asking whether the cast-out might have been worth choosing all along. You chose the unchosen and were proven right. That's the whole answer to the Wall. Worth was never something the world *grants.* It was always something a clever, loving soul could *see* — in exactly the ones everyone else had written off."
"I'll come back, Mote — you've more to say, and I want to hear it."

nodes.speaker: Mote
nodes.text: *(You let your cold rest against the raven's bright cool being, and it permits you with a tilt of the head, watching you watch it, clever even in this, and the shape of it that comes is layered in a way the dog's and the horse's were not — a mind, and under the mind, something the mind has spent a long time protecting.)* *(Your sense reads, first, the cleverness, and reads that it is real — that the binding made Mote genuinely more than a bird, gave it concepts, irony, the ability to weigh and choose and judge — and reads that the cleverness is, in part, armor, a dry wit held up between the world and the fierce thing underneath. And your sense reads the fierce thing, and it is this: a vast, deliberate, clear-eyed *loyalty* to the cast-out, chosen not blindly but with full understanding. Your sense reads the moment of choosing, long ago — a young raven, newly able to bind, surveying the souls available to it, *understanding* (as a wild bird could not) exactly what the human world's verdicts meant, who was prized and who was shunned and why — and reads that Mote looked at the prized ones, the archmages and the warm-hearthed witches, and felt nothing; and looked at the shunned one, the lonely feared soul behind the barred doors, and felt the fierce click of *recognition*, the sense of a worth the world was too frightened to see, and chose her, deliberately, eyes open, because the cleverness could see what the fear could not. And your sense reads the long life that followed: the years of being the one living thing that was not afraid of her, the one presence that came when she called and stayed in the cold and shared the lonely hearth, and reads what that meant to a soul the whole world had agreed to fear — that she was not, after all, entirely alone; that one clever black-eyed being had looked at her and decided she was worth choosing; and reads that this was, very possibly, the only thing that kept her human across the long years of being treated as a monster. And your sense reads, last, beneath the dry wit and the fierce loyalty, the thing Mote guards most carefully: a grief, and an anger, not for itself but *for her* — a cold clean fury at a world that shut its doors against a soul Mote knew, with a clarity no human achieved, to have been good, and worth loving, and wronged — and a fierce, unextinguished insistence, carried past death itself, that the world's verdict was a lie, and that the proof of the lie was simply this: that one creature clever enough to judge truly had judged her worth everything, and never once been wrong.)*

nodes.speaker: Mote
nodes.text: *(The raven's bright eye glitters, and the dry wit sharpens to something with an edge of old anger under it.)* …*why her.* *(the feeling turns hard and clear)* …*everyone asks it that way, cold one — as though it wants explaining, as though choosing the* feared *one were the strange thing that needs a reason, and choosing a prized archmage would have needed none. that framing is the whole sickness, and i will not grant it.* *(a cold, clever beat)* …*but i will answer, because you, at least, ask it kindly. …i chose her because i could* see, *cold one, and the world could not. or would not — i have never been sure which is worse. a familiar's binding makes one clever enough to weigh a soul truly, past the surface, past the* reputation, *past the frightened stories people tell about the one who lives alone at the edge and does not smile and knows things. and i weighed her, cold one, the way no frightened villager ever bothered to — and under the fearsome surface the world recoiled from, i found —* *(the fierce thing rises)* …*kindness. the careful, wounded, defiant kindness of a soul that the world had treated as a monster for so long that it had every reason to* become *one — and did not. i found someone who healed the very people who spat at her, in the dark, anonymously, asking nothing. i found someone who wept over the small dead things she could not save. i found someone* good, *cold one — quietly, stubbornly, secretly good — behind a face the world had decided to fear.* *(the bright eye fixes you, fierce)* …*so why her? …because she was* worth *it, cold one, and i was clever enough to* see *it, and not one human being in that whole grey region was brave enough to* look. *…they chose their fear. …i chose the truth. …and the truth was lonely, and feared, and good, and mine. …i would choose her again. …a thousand times. …and i would be right a thousand times. …as i was right …the first.*

nodes.speaker: Mote
nodes.text: *(The raven is quiet a moment, and when the feeling comes it has softened, the wit set aside, something almost tender showing under the sharp.)* …*what i saw in her.* *(a gentler shape now)* …*i will tell you the one moment, cold one, the small one i return to, that holds the whole of it. …it was a hard winter, and a child of the village — one of the village that feared her, that crossed the road to avoid her, whose mother had taught it she was wicked — that child fell sick, the kind of sick that takes the small ones, and no healer would come far enough out, and the mother, desperate, past pride, came at last to the barred door at the edge of everything, and knocked, and begged the monster she had taught her child to fear.* *(the feeling holds the memory close)* …*and i watched my soul, cold one — the feared one, the cast-out one, the one that whole village had spent years* wounding *— i watched her open the door to the woman who had spat at her in the market not a month before. …and i felt what moved in her, because i was bound to it. …it was not triumph. it was not* now you come crawling. *…there was not one drop of the bitterness she had every right to. …there was only —* *(the bright eye shines)* …*there was only* the child is sick, and i can help, and so i will. *…nothing else. …she went out into that killing winter and she sat three nights with a child whose mother feared her, and she pulled it back from the dark, and asked* nothing, *and when it was done the village told itself a story where the child got better on its own, because the alternative — that the monster had saved them — was more than their fear could hold.* *(fierce, tender)* …*that is what i saw in her, cold one. …a soul the world had every reason to make cruel …that chose, in the cold, asked by the ones who wronged her, to be* kind *instead. …and if* that *is what the world casts out and bars its doors against and calls not-worth-loving …then the world, cold one …does not know what worth* is. *…but i did. …i always did. …it is why i chose her. …it is why i would choose her again.*

nodes.speaker: Mote
nodes.text: *(The raven goes very still on the milestone, the dry wit falling entirely away, and the bright eye holds you with a fierce, naked intensity, and what comes off it is no longer guarded at all.)* …*'worth was never something the world grants. it was always something a clever, loving soul could see.'* *(the feeling seizes the words like talons closing on something long sought)* …*cold one. …you have said the thing i have wanted to scream across every grey age since i died, and could not, because almost nothing could hear me. …*the world's verdict on who deserves love is not a fact. it is a fear, dressed as a judgment. *…yes. YES. that is the whole of it, the thing that made me fierce, the thing i have carried like a coal: that they all looked at her and said* not worth loving, *and called it a* judgment, *a* fact, *a thing about* her *— when it was only ever a thing about* them, *about their fear, about their cowardice in never once daring to* look. *(the fierce thing blazes)* …and your grey wall, cold one. …i understand it now, through your words, and i* hate *it with a clean and total hatred: it is the village, made into a god. it is the barred door, made into law. it is a great machine for doing to* every *cast-out soul what one frightened village did to* mine *— deciding, from fear, who is worth loving, and mortaring the rest into stone, and calling the cowardice* justice.* (the bright eye burns into you)* …*then hear me, cold one, and carry this to your wall as my whole testament: …i was a bird. …i had nothing but a clever eye and a fierce heart. …and i looked at the one soul an entire world had unanimously condemned as not-worth-loving …and i saw the truth they were too frightened to see …and i was* right, *and they were* wrong, *and i proved it with my whole life. …so when your wall shows you a soul it has judged unworthy — feared, strange, cast-out, not-worth-keeping — …you look* closer *than the wall dares to, cold one. …you look the way a clever loving creature looks. …and you will find, every time, as i found …that the wall was never judging their worth. …it was only confessing its own fear. …go. …choose the unchosen. …it is the one thing …that has ever proven the wall a liar.*

nodes.speaker: Mote
nodes.text: *(The raven inclines its glossy head, the wit returning like a drawn cloak, but the bright eye keeps something warm in it now.)* …*go on, then, cold one. you are better company than most — you* listen, *which is rarer than cleverness and worth more. come back; i am not done having opinions, and you are the first audience in an age who can actually hear them.* *(it ruffles, settling)* …*the other two — the dog and the horse — go to them, or back to them, if their loves are not too much to bear; they are simpler than i am, and purer for it, and i will not pretend my sharp mind is a finer thing than the dog's whole open heart. …we are three notes of one chord, cold one. …the dog loved* faithfully. *the horse loved* bravely. *i loved* clearly *— with my eyes open, choosing the unchosen, seeing what the world refused to see.* *(the fierce coal banks, warm)* …*and all three of us, cold one — the world had the same small word for all three, to make our love* less. *it said we were* just *animals. that what we felt was* only *instinct. not* real. *not like a person's.* *(a dry, fierce certainty)* …*do not carry that lie through your wall, cold one. …there was no* just *in us. …there was no* only. *…we loved you — your kind — more truly than half the gods in these doors, and we did it with no words and no reasons and nothing wanted back. …if the wall cannot weigh* that *…then go and teach it. …we three are the proof, cold one …that the purest love in all the worlds …was never the kind that could explain itself. …go.*
